nativeRange.habitat: Marine demersal; inhabits sandy and rubble-strewn reef areas at depths of roughly 6–52 m

decorPreferences: live rock rubble for den construction

diet.notes: Generally fed a variety of meaty foods including marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, and mysis shrimp. Has been reported to readily accept frozen preparations. Tends to feed from the entrance of its burrow, snapping at passing prey.

summary: A recently described Indo-Pacific jawfish with a distinctive black chin band, known for its burrowing behavior and generally peaceful disposition. Generally considered moderate difficulty due to substrate and escape-prevention requirements.
careNotes:
The Chinstrap Jawfish (Opistognathus wassi) was formally described by Smith-Vaniz in 2023 and is still sold under the trade name 'Opistognathus sp 6' or 'Opistognathus sp.' by some retailers. It is recognized by conspicuous large pale spots along the body, a prominent ocellus (eyespot) on the dorsal fin, and the distinctive black band extending from the lower jaw to the chin that gives it its common name.

This species tends to construct burrows in the substrate and will rearrange rubble, shells, and substrate material to fortify its den. A minimum of 7–10 cm (3–4 inches) of mixed substrate (fine sand combined with coarser rubble and shell fragments) is generally advised to allow natural burrowing behavior. Rocks and broken coral branches placed near the substrate have been noted as helpful den-reinforcement material.

Jawfish are well-documented jumpers and have been known to leap from aquariums, particularly when startled or newly introduced. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended. Escape risk is rated high for this species.

Generally peaceful with most reef inhabitants, but has been known to be territorial toward conspecifics. Only one specimen should be kept per tank unless a mated pair is available, and the tank should be large enough to provide distinct burrow territories if keeping a pair. May occasionally pose a minor threat to very small ornamental shrimp.

Scientific name confirmed via FishBase (O. wassi, Smith-Vaniz, 2023); LiveAquaria still uses the pre-description trade designation 'Opistognathus sp 6'. Temperature and salinity parameters sourced from LiveAquaria, consistent with the genus as a whole. No independent temperature data found on FishBase for this species — parameters treated as single-source (LiveAquaria), but consistent with all other jawfish in the genus.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The flagged concern was single-source temperature data (LiveAquaria only; 22.2–25.6°C / 72–78°F). FishBase was re-checked and confirms no species-specific temperature data exists for O. wassi. However, the congener O. aurifrons (Yellowhead Jawfish) is listed on FishBase at 22–28°C, and the genus as a whole occupies tropical reef waters — the LiveAquaria range (22.2–25.6°C) falls within and is consistent with this genus-level envelope. Wikipedia returns no article for O. wassi; BlueZoo Aquatics was unreachable (TLS error); ReefApp has no entry for this species. No second source contradicts the LiveAquaria parameters. The single-source disclosure is preserved in careNotes as a transparency note. Additionally, minSandDepthCm was populated (set to 8 cm) from the LiveAquaria care guideline of 3–4 inches (7.6–10 cm) of mixed substrate, resolving the null field. fish.mouthbrooder confirmed true (paternal oral incubation, consistent with genus).
breedingNotes: Jawfish are reported to be mouthbrooders, with males typically incubating the egg mass in their mouths. Spawning has been documented in captivity for related species in the genus (notably the Yellowhead Jawfish). Captive breeding of the Chinstrap Jawfish specifically has not been widely documented in available hobbyist literature as of 2026.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name as Opistognathus wassi (Smith-Vaniz, 2023). Provided adult size (6.2 cm SL), native range countries, depth range (6–52 m), family, and order. Temperature/pH/salinity data not available on FishBase for this species — sourced from LiveAquaria only.

fish.breedingNotes: Mouthbrooding genus; males incubate eggs orally. Captive breeding has been documented for related jawfish species but is not widely reported for O. wassi specifically.
